stage.name: Checkpoint 04: frontend decisions without guessing
stage.description: A choice-only checkpoint that validates PC selection, replay, exception redirects, RAS, BHT, BTB caveats, and source-scoped frontend claims.
stage.courseTemplateName: Professional Technical Deep Dive - Evidence-Grounded SlideKit

content.title: Checkpoint 04: frontend decisions without guessing
content.subtitle: This checkpoint asks whether you can place PC sources, replay, RAS, BHT, BTB, and redirects without relying on vague intuition.
content.thesis: Passing this checkpoint means you can explain the frontend as a set of source-backed decisions, not as a black box that somehow fetches instructions.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: PC Generation stage
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: PC gen selects the next PC from reset, branch prediction, default fetch, mispredict correction, replay, exception/interrupt, return from exception, or commit-PC restart cases.

content.speakerNotes: This is the Unit 04 checkpoint. The goal is not to make you recite every signal. The goal is to check whether you can place each frontend behavior in the right bucket: PC selection, replay, RAS, BHT, BTB caveat, exception redirect, and flush-sensitive correction. If you can do that, frontend deep dives become manageable.

actions.text: This is the Unit 04 checkpoint. The goal is not to make you recite every signal. The goal is to check whether you can place each frontend behavior in the right bucket: PC selection, replay, RAS, BHT, BTB caveat, exception redirect, and flush-sensitive correction. If you can do that, frontend deep dives become manageable.

content.title: Six ideas, six different jobs
content.subtitle: The checkpoint is mostly a test of not mixing roles.

cards.title: Replay
cards.body: Refetches when queue capacity or tracking requires it.

cards.title: RAS
cards.body: Predicts return targets with a two-entry LIFO.

cards.title: Redirects
cards.body: Mispredict, exception, eret, and commit paths restart the trustworthy stream.

content.takeaway: A correct answer should place the behavior before explaining it.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: PC Generation stage
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: PC gen selects the next PC from reset, branch prediction, default fetch, mispredict correction, replay, exception/interrupt, return from exception, or commit-PC restart cases.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: RAS submodule
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: RAS is a two-entry LIFO; call JAL pushes the following PC, return JALR pops a predicted return address, wrong prediction can generate a mispredict, and RAS is never flushed.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: BHT submodule
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The BHT has 32 entries, stores valid bits and two-bit saturating counters, predicts taken/not taken from the counter MSB, is updated by Execute, and is never flushed.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: BTB submodule
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The CV32A60X configuration has no BTB, so no valid address is returned from BTB.

content.speakerNotes: Here is the map to hold in your head. PC gen selects the next address source. Replay refetches work safely. RAS predicts returns. BHT predicts branch direction. BTB is absent in CV32A60X. Redirects restart the trustworthy instruction stream. That is a lot, but it is organized. Organization is doing half the teaching work.

actions.text: Here is the map to hold in your head. PC gen selects the next address source. Replay refetches work safely. RAS predicts returns. BHT predicts branch direction. BTB is absent in CV32A60X. Redirects restart the trustworthy instruction stream. That is a lot, but it is organized. Organization is doing half the teaching work.

content.title: Do not publish slides with these mistakes
content.subtitle: These are the errors this checkpoint is designed to catch.

items.title: PC plus four only
items.body: Ignoring prediction, replay, exception, return, and commit-PC restart paths.

items.title: Prediction equals correction
items.body: Blurring frontend guesses with execute or CSR-provided redirects.

items.title: BHT equals BTB
items.body: Confusing direction prediction with target-address prediction.

items.title: Generic CVA6 overclaim
items.body: Forgetting that CV32A60X has no BTB even if family-level vocabulary mentions BTB.

items.title: No source anchor
items.body: Making a public architecture claim without tying it to the official design text.

content.takeaway: The checkpoint is a publication filter as much as a learner quiz.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: PC Generation stage
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: PC gen selects the next PC from reset, branch prediction, default fetch, mispredict correction, replay, exception/interrupt, return from exception, or commit-PC restart cases.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: BHT submodule
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The BHT has 32 entries, stores valid bits and two-bit saturating counters, predicts taken/not taken from the counter MSB, is updated by Execute, and is never flushed.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: BTB submodule
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The CV32A60X configuration has no BTB, so no valid address is returned from BTB.

content.speakerNotes: These are the anti-patterns we are trying to remove from the course pipeline. Do not reduce PC generation to PC plus four. Do not merge prediction with correction. Do not merge BHT with BTB. Do not overclaim generic CVA6 behavior when CV32A60X says otherwise. And do not publish architecture claims without source anchors. Future Alex will thank us. Probably with coffee.

actions.text: These are the anti-patterns we are trying to remove from the course pipeline. Do not reduce PC generation to PC plus four. Do not merge prediction with correction. Do not merge BHT with BTB. Do not overclaim generic CVA6 behavior when CV32A60X says otherwise. And do not publish architecture claims without source anchors. Future Alex will thank us. Probably with coffee.

questions.question: Which next-PC source is tied to instruction queue capacity pressure?
questions.analysis: Replay is requested by the instruction queue when FIFO capacity or prediction-address tracking prevents safe acceptance.

questions.question: Which component predicts return targets in CV32A60X?
questions.analysis: RAS is the return address stack and is used for return-like control flow.

questions.question: Which component provides taken/not-taken direction prediction for conditional branches?
questions.analysis: BHT provides direction prediction using valid bits and two-bit counters.

questions.question: What is the safe CV32A60X statement about BTB?

options.label: There is no BTB in CV32A60X
BTB is a two-entry return stack
BTB replaces the BHT whenever compressed instructions are enabled

questions.analysis: The BTB submodule section explicitly says there is no BTB in CV32A60X.

questions.question: Which paths can redirect or restart frontend fetching?
questions.analysis: All four are part of the PC generation or redirect story in the frontend/control material.

questions.question: Which statements show careful source-scoped wording?

options.label: RAS has two entries in CV32A60X
Replay is a queue-driven refetch mechanism rather than a BHT direction guess

questions.analysis: All four statements preserve source scope and keep component roles separate.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: PC Generation stage
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: PC gen selects the next PC from reset, branch prediction, default fetch, mispredict correction, replay, exception/interrupt, return from exception, or commit-PC restart cases.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: instr_queue submodule
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The instruction queue stores instructions, addresses, control-flow type, exceptions, and predicted addresses; it requests replay when instruction or address FIFO capacity is exceeded.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: RAS submodule
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: RAS is a two-entry LIFO; call JAL pushes the following PC, return JALR pops a predicted return address, wrong prediction can generate a mispredict, and RAS is never flushed.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: BHT submodule
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The BHT has 32 entries, stores valid bits and two-bit saturating counters, predicts taken/not taken from the counter MSB, is updated by Execute, and is never flushed.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: BTB submodule
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The CV32A60X configuration has no BTB, so no valid address is returned from BTB.

content.speakerNotes: These questions are deliberately choice-only so the public classroom remains locally gradable. They also double as a publishing checklist. If a future lesson cannot answer these questions cleanly, it probably should not move forward to final audio and deployment yet.

actions.text: These questions are deliberately choice-only so the public classroom remains locally gradable. They also double as a publishing checklist. If a future lesson cannot answer these questions cleanly, it probably should not move forward to final audio and deployment yet.

content.title: You can now read deeper frontend material with discipline
content.subtitle: The next units can add cache, decode, hazard, and backend detail without losing the control-flow spine.
takeaways: PC generation is a source-priority decision, not a fixed increment.
Replay and redirect paths restart trustworthy frontend work.
RAS, BHT, and BTB-style target prediction must remain distinct and configuration-scoped.
content.nextStep: Next course direction: either go deeper into fetch realignment/instruction queue behavior, or move to decode and branch-resolution interfaces.
content.closing: Unit 04 gives us the frontend grammar. From here, deeper RTL reading can be technical without becoming chaotic.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: PC Generation stage
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: PC gen selects the next PC from reset, branch prediction, default fetch, mispredict correction, replay, exception/interrupt, return from exception, or commit-PC restart cases.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: Fetch stage
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: Fetch requests 32-bit aligned blocks from cache, realigns instructions, pushes entries into the instruction queue, attaches predicted addresses, and forwards cache exceptions to decode.

content.speakerNotes: Unit 04 gives us the frontend grammar. PC generation chooses among sources. Replay and redirects keep the stream trustworthy. RAS, BHT, and BTB-style ideas are separate, and CV32A60X has the no-BTB caveat. From here, we can go deeper into fetch realignment and instruction queue behavior, or follow branch resolution into execute. Either path is now grounded.

actions.text: Unit 04 gives us the frontend grammar. PC generation chooses among sources. Replay and redirects keep the stream trustworthy. RAS, BHT, and BTB-style ideas are separate, and CV32A60X has the no-BTB caveat. From here, we can go deeper into fetch realignment and instruction queue behavior, or follow branch resolution into execute. Either path is now grounded.
